尽管Shakir似乎很适合这家以博士和丰富的投资而闻名的公司，但正如她上周在一次漫长的采访中告诉我们的那样，创业并不是她专注于职业的事情。夏奇尔是一名伊拉克裔美国人，父母随父母移居该地区，然后在硅谷长大。她凭借优异的奖学金和工作经历，同时进入了哈佛大学和乔治敦外交学院，并认为自己可能会成为一名医生或获得博士学位。在人类学中。
 取而代之的是，在英国广播公司（BBC）的实习中，她掩盖了白宫历史性的演讲，这使她在国务院工作了两年多，为当时的克林顿秘书工作。后来她回家与家人同住时，她意识到自己想产生影响。她没有预料到自己会通过投资来实现这一目标，但这就是正在发生的事情。
  如果您不认识Shakir，则值得在这里聆听我们的谈话；同时，这里有一些经过精心编辑的摘录，这些摘录对她下注的位置以及原因进行了说明。
 TC：勒克斯以投资火箭公司和卫星而闻名，尽管其网站实际上列出了其投资组合中的21个不同行业。公司的交易是来自对公司感兴趣的这些行业，还是由于团队对公司感兴趣并且恰好在这些行业中运作？
 DS：实际上，我发现某些行业分类是有问题的，因为如果仔细看产品组合，您会发现我们喜欢医疗保健，肉类，机器人技术，人工智能，食品生产，工业物联网以及符合FinTech的数据。就我们如何对待行业而言，这确实是交叉的。这就是我们如何思考投资的本质。但就我们如何考虑在何处进行投资而言，我们当然在某些领域上历来花费了很多时间，这些领域使我们洞悉了空白区域（例如在自动驾驶汽车上工作）和通过这些经验，可以洞察可以推动下一代自治的软件平台类型。因此，肯定会有很多世代相传的投资。
 TC：上周，勒克斯参加了名为Varda的初创公司种子轮融资，该公司以太空制造为中心。它试图在太空中制造什么，为什么？
 DS：我认为很少有公司比Varda更像是勒克斯。我们以能够将科幻小说变成事实（包括在太空中）的公司而感到自豪。我们在相对论空间中；我们在许多卫星公司中-Orbital Insight和Planet以及其他几家公司。 
但这不仅是我们感兴趣的空间，而且创始团队也是我们有机会随着时间的流逝而了解并非常感动的一个空间。这不是一家直观的公司，这可能是您可能会问它的部分原因，例如：他们到底在做什么，对吗？归根结底，还为时过早，但是他们正在研究我们真正喜欢的两件事-制造和太空-以及一些真正有趣的创新，我们才刚刚开始了解它们在太空中的应用（包括周围的）物理过程和供应链。而这个团队处于接管这些人才的最前沿。
 TC：是否会在太空中制造碳纳米管带到国际空间站？有什么需要解决的？
 DK：归根结底，需求围绕成本和供应链。将任何物体送入轨道仍然非常昂贵-我们正在谈论数千万美元，甚至更多。这就是他们要解决的关键问题。这里的假设是太空制造。在这一点上，他们对于如何能够做到这一点有一些有趣而机密的假设。
 TC：对于需要大量资金的公司而言，是否有足够的后期资金？ Lux去年筹集了10亿美元的资金后，目前管理着25亿美元的资产。但是一些太空投资者说，[没有足够的余地]部分是因为还没有足够的流动性事件。
 DK：这很有趣，因为我们对现在称为前沿技术的技术感到非常兴奋，因为在那之前，它真的是一类，尤其是在它还没有任何风险投资回报之前。现在，越来越明显的是前沿技术不再处于前沿。它已成为许多大型企业的重要组成部分。而且，我们看到了大规模生产火箭的大公司。我们看到私人公司将火箭发射到太空并与NASA合作。我们还看到了以前听起来像科幻小说的风险投资公司，而且它们的估值都非常惊人。看来我们现在处在一个转折点。如果您查看这些类型公司的上限表，您会发现玩家不一定早些时候就进行了这些类型的下注。
 TC：您的选择之一就是Shiru，它正在利用计算设计来创建增强的蛋白质，以帮助养活整个世界。它对您的兴趣和过程有何评价？
 DS：我花了很多时间，尤其是在GV，与我们投资组合中的一些替代蛋白质公司合作。自从我上任以来，以及我在Google的整个工作期间，我所做的大部分工作一直在与商业伙伴，《财富》 500强公司进行交流，并了解他们的需求。我在GV工作的很大一部分是帮助新兴的科技公司与这些大型，庞然大物的公司建立联系，并帮助它们满足他们的创新需求，而这正是我的论文的主要来源。 
我真诚地相信，食品中存在着巨大的机会，这些财富500强食品公司可以满足消费者不断增长的需求，环境需求以及与动物成分有关的成本需求。 Shiru的方法很有趣，因为他们采用的业务模式在制药业表现良好。 。使新型IP的生产成为可能。在这种情况下，它是基于植物的新型蛋白质，它使用机器学习和计算生物学技术，并实际上将其许可给了这些食品公司，以便他们可以继续生产自己的不可能的汉堡或超越肉类的食品，或仅替换昂贵的动物他们的产品之一中添加了基于维生素的成分。
 TC：您的另一笔交易是AllStripes，它汇总并分析病历，然后将已识别的数据出售给制药公司，以帮助他们开发药物。那么通用线程是机器学习吗？
 DS：是的，对我而言，这是关于机器学习AI，它正在精简一个真正的模拟行业，无论是教育，金融，食品还是医疗保健，我都将大部分时间都花在了那里。
  DK：特别是在医疗保健方面，我花了大量时间在女性健康和女性健康方面。我在生育技术上看了很多，从机器人冷冻储存器到胚胎选择，再到体外受精的消费化诊所，我看到了各种各样的东西。这绝对是一个有趣的市场，并且正在增长，特别是如果您更广泛地关注人口统计学方面的趋势，即女性在晚些时候生孩子，以及我们在IVF率和其他国家/地区所看到的。
 我是两个孩子的母亲，我两个孩子的出生时都经历过非常可怕的医疗经历，这无疑激发了我对患者这一领域的看法，但我也确实认为投资不足。当我听到有人谈论女性健康作为利基市场时，我感到非常沮丧，这没有什么利基的。从纯数字的角度来看，[妇女]显然占人口的一半。但是女性也占了医疗保健支出的80％以上，因此在医疗保健领域专注于女性的公司在其他领域也很出色。
 因此，在女性健康领域之外，我正在研究更年期和年龄跨性别人群的年龄。心理健康也很重要。我来自一个医师家庭。我父亲是精神科医生。实际上，这就是我在湾区长大的原因。他在70年代来到斯坦福大学（Stanford）从事精神病学研究，对精神健康一生着迷。实际上，就像很多移民的孩子一样，我很想念自己，自己会当医生，甚至在上大学之前就曾在VA的精神病学部门实习。
 我对此感到兴奋的部分原因是，它仍然是医学界尚未受到技术影响的领域之一-当然，就数据方面而言，采用电子病历的速度很慢。在诊断和治疗方面，有一些非常有趣的非药物干预心理健康干预措施，例如TMS（经颅磁刺激），但它难以捉摸，当然，对于许多人来说，它现在已成为人们的首要考虑传染给所有人的心理健康负担。因此，我们看到了许多非常有趣的新颖方法出现，以及顺风推动了一些在太空中待了一段时间的公司。 
TC：Lux实际上在10月就成立了3.45亿美元的专注于医疗保健的SPAC或特殊目的收购公司。 这是未来的众多选择之一，还是支持Lux在较早时候投资的公司的想法，还是支持与Lux没有任何关联的公司？  DS：我们相信，对于那些有令人振奋的大故事可以讲述并不一定能通过后视镜评估其价值的公司而言，SPAC确实具有强大的功能。 考虑到医疗保健性质的不断变化，特别是考虑到大流行病已经发生的一切，这是一个特别重要且有趣的医疗保健机会。  我们已经从医疗保健领域的一些顶级公司那里获得了一些非常有趣的信息。 您可以想象，这是医疗保健非常非常有趣的时刻。 我们不是在寻找自己的投资组合公司。 但是我们的许多公司都走这条路，我们鼓励他们[追求]。
note_en:
While Shakir seems to fit right in with the firm — known for its wealth of PhDs and moonshot investments — venture wasn’t something she was focused on as a career, as she told us during a lengthy interview last week. An Iraqi-American who grew up in Silicon Valley after her parents immigrated to the region, Shakir put herself through both Harvard, then Georgetown’s School of Foreign Service through a mix of merit scholarships and work and thought she might become a doctor or nab a PhD in anthropology.
 Instead, an internship with the BBC saw her cover an historic White House speech, which led her to spend more than two years at the State Department, working for then Secretary Clinton. When she later headed home to be with her family, she was aware that she wanted to make an impact; she didn’t foresee making it through her investments, yet that’s what’s now happening.
  If you don’t know Shakir, it’s worth listening to our conversation  here at some point; in the meantime, here are some lightly edited excerpts that shine a light on where she is placing her bets and why.
 TC: Lux is known for investing in rocket companies and satellites, though its website actually lists 21 different industries in its portfolio. Did the firm’s deals arise from these industries that interest the firm or because of teams that interest the firm and that happen to operate in these industries?
 DS: I actually find some of this industry categorization as problematic because if you look through the portfolio, you’ll see we love healthcare, meats, robotics, AI, food production, industrial IoT, and data meets FinTech. It really is intersectional in terms of how we approach industries; that’s the nature of how we think about investing. But in terms of how we think about where we want to make investments, we certainly have areas where historically we’ve spent a lot of time that have given us insights into where there are white spaces [such as having worked on] autonomous vehicles and having insights through that experience into the types of software platforms that could power the next generation of autonomy. So there’s definitely a lot of generational and evolutionary investing.
 TC: Last week, Lux participated in the  seed round a startup called Varda that is centered around manufacturing in space. What is it trying to make in space and why?
 DS: There are few companies that I think would be perceived as more of a Lux fit than Varda. We pride ourselves on companies that turns science fiction into fact [including in the] space space. We were in Relativity Space; we’re in a number of satellite companies — Orbital Insight and Planet and several others.
 But this is not only a space that we’re interested in, but also the founding team is one that we’ve had the chance to get to know over time and felt very strongly about. It is not an intuitive company, which is probably part of the reason why you might be asking about it, like: what exactly do they do, right? At the end of the day, it’s quite early, but they’re working on two things that we really love — manufacturing and space — abd there are some really interesting innovations that we’re just at the very beginning of understanding their application in space [including around] physical processes and the supply chain. And this team is at the forefront of taking those on.
 TC: Is it going to be manufacturing carbon nanotubes in space to take to the ISS? What’s the need it’s addressing?
 DK: At the end of the day, the need is around cost and supply chain. It’s still extremely expensive to launch anything into orbit — we’re talking tens of millions of dollars if not more. That’s the key problem that they want to solve. Manufacturing in space is the hypothesis here. And they have some fascinating and confidential, at this point, hypotheses as to how they will be able to do that.
 TC: Is there enough later-stage funding for companies that will need a lot of capital? Lux is now managing $2.5 billion in assets, after raising $1 billion last year. But some space investors say there is not [enough to go around] partly because there haven’t yet been enough liquidity events.
 DK: That’s interesting, because we got excited about what we now call frontier tech before that was really a category — certainly before it had any venture-return profile. Now, it’s becoming increasingly clear that frontier tech is no longer at the frontier. It’s becoming a key part of many large enterprises. And we’re seeing huge companies producing rockets en masse. We’re seeing private companies launch rockets into space and collaborate with NASA. We’re also seeing venture-funded companies that previously sounded like science fiction that are approaching just phenomenal valuations. So it does seem like we’re at a turning point here. If you take a look at the cap tables [of] these types of companies, you’ll see players that may not necessarily have taken those types of bets earlier on.
 TC: One of your bets is  Shiru, which is leveraging computational design to create enhanced proteins to help feed the world. What does it say about your interests and process?
 DS: I spent a lot of time, especially at GV, with some of the alternative protein companies in our portfolio. And a big part of what I’ve done since my days in government, and certainly throughout my time at Google, has been talking to commercial partners, Fortune 500 companies, and understanding what their needs are. A big part of my role at GV was helping to plug in these large, behemoth companies with up-and-coming tech companies and help them to meet the innovation demands that they have, and that’s a large part of where my thesis came from.
 I genuinely believe that there is a huge opportunity in food that enables these Fortune 500 food companies to meet the increasing demands from consumers, environmental demands, and cost demands [related to] animal-based ingredients. Shiru’s approach is an interesting one because they are taking a business model that has worked quite well in pharma . . .in enabling the production of novel IP. In this case, [it’s] novel plant-based proteins, using machine learning and computational biology and actually licensing it to these food companies so they can go on to produce their own versions of the Impossible Burger or Beyond Meat or simply replace a costly animal-based ingredient in one of their products.
 TC: Another of your deals is  AllStripes, which aggregates and analyzes medical records, then sells the de identified data to pharma companies to help them develop medicines. So the common thread is machine learning?
 DS: Yes, so for me, it’s about machine learning AI that’s streamlining a really analog industry, whether it’s education, whether it’s finance, whether it’s food, and certainly healthcare, where I spend the majority of my time.
  DK: Within healthcare, specifically, I am spending a lot of time looking at women’s health and within women’s health. I am looking a lot at fertility tech, and I’ve seen everything from robotic cryo storage to embryo selection to consumerized clinics for IVF. And it’s definitely an interesting market and one that’s growing, especially if you look more broadly at demographic trends in terms of women having children later in life, as well as what we’re seeing in terms of IVF rates and other countries as well.
 I’m a mother of two young kids and had some pretty scary medical experiences myself with with both of their births, and that definitely inspired a look into that space as a patient, but also one where I think there’s really been underinvestment. I get really frustrated when I hear anyone talk about women’s health as niche, there is nothing niche about it. From the pure numbers perspective, [women] represents obviously half the population. But women also account for over 80% of dollars spent in healthcare and so companies that are focused on women in the healthcare space are also excellent wedges into other areas.
 So outside of those areas in women’s health, I’m looking at menopause and at aging in place for anyone across the gender spectrum. Mental health is a huge one, too. I come from a family of physicians. My father is a psychiatrist. In fact, that’s the reason I grew up in the Bay Area. He came to Stanford in the ’70s for his residency in psychiatry and I’ve had a lifelong fascination with mental health. In fact, like many children of immigrants, for a hot minute I thought I was going to be a doctor myself and and even interned with VA’s psychiatry department right before going to college.
 Part of why I get excited about it is because it is one of the fields in medicine that’s still so untouched by tech — certainly on the data side in terms of being slow to adopt EHRs. On the diagnostic and therapeutic side, there have been some really interesting non-pharmacological interventions for mental health, like TMS (transcranial magnetic stimulation), but it has been elusive to attack and, of course, it’s top of mind for many folks right now with the mental health burden on everyone at the pandemic. So we’re seeing a lot of really interesting novel approaches emerge, as well as tailwinds propelling some companies that have been around for a while in a space.
 TC: Lux actually formed a $345 million  health-care focused SPAC, or special purpose acquisition company, in October. Is that one of many to come and also, is the idea to perhaps back a company that Lux has funded earlier in time or an outfit it has no association with whatsoever?
 DS: We believe that SPACs are really powerful for companies that have an exciting big story to tell and whose value isn’t necessarily best evaluated by looking in the rearview mirror. And given the changing nature of health care, particularly given everything the pandemic has done, it’s a particularly salient and interesting opportunity for health care.
 We’ve have gotten some  really interesting inbound from some of the top companies in the healthcare space. As you can imagine, it’s just a very, very interesting time for healthcare. We’re not looking at our own portfolio companies. But a number of our companies are going down that path, and we’re encouraging them to [pursue it].

我们必须与酱油水坑，无序的食物堆，以及最重要的是，对抗松弛的，抗病毒的意大利面。诗人Filippo Tommaso Marinetti
 未来主义之父，奉献的挑衅者菲利波·汤玛索·马里尼蒂（Filippo Tommaso Marinetti）感到很奇怪，他将发现他最令人兴奋的美食宣言与当今低碳水化合物十字军的观点有多么紧密的联系。
 在谴责“那是荒谬的意大利美食宗教”的面食时，他的意图是震惊和批评资产阶级，而不是减轻膨胀和发炎。
 但是，他确实同意21世纪流行的观点，即浓重的意大利面饭会让食客感到同样沉重和嗜睡。
   未来主义的烹饪将摆脱对体积和重量的古老迷恋，并将废除意大利面作为其原则之一。 Pastasciutta是一种Pa​​sséist美食，因为它使人沉重，野蛮，使他们觉得自己有营养，使他们持怀疑态度，缓慢，悲观……任何食膏主义者都会在吞食他的二等分食物时诚实地检查自己的良心。面食金字塔会在阻止黑洞的令人沮丧的满足感中找到。这个空洞是他无法治愈的悲伤。他可能会自欺欺人，但是没有什么可以填补它。只有未来主义者的一餐才能振作精神。面食具有抗病毒作用，因为胃肿大，不鼓舞女人的身体热情，也不喜欢随时随地拥有她。
  炸弹自然来了。虽然他真正相信未来主义的宗旨-速度，工业，技术和战争的清洗效果，却以牺牲传统和过去为代价-但他却夸张地夸张，荒唐和胡闹。
 未来主义食谱》反映了这一点，尽管它确实包含了实际的食谱，并针对如何配菜提供了非常具体的说明。一个样品： 
小号高炉炸生肉：切成块完美的牛肉。使电流通过，然后在朗姆酒，白兰地和白色苦艾酒的混合物中浸泡24小时。从混合物中将其除去，并在红辣椒，黑胡椒和雪床上食用。每口要仔细地咀嚼一分钟，然后每口都被食者本人吹小号的剧烈冲击分开。
 无畏的主持人特雷弗·邓瑟思（Trevor Dunseith）在上面的视频中记录了他试图举办一场忠实的未来主义晚宴的企图。
 客人可以用手吃沙拉，以最大程度地获得“阴唇前的触觉愉悦”，然后平衡头上装有开胃菜的橙子，以随机选择每个满口。尽管并非所有口味都受到欢迎，但聚会人士同意，这种体验是（按预期）完全新颖（并且没有100％的意大利面食）。
 马里内蒂（Marinetti）的反义大利运动与总理贝尼托·墨索里尼（Benito Mussolini）消除意大利对外国市场的经济依赖的目标（谷物之战）一致。北方农民可以生产充足的大米，但远远不足以支撑平民的意大利面消费。如果意大利人无法种更多的小麦，墨索里尼希望他们从面食转向大米。
   Marinetti同意大米将是“爱国”的选择，但他想要的目标植根于他自己的前卫艺术运动中：
 ……这不仅仅是用大米代替意大利面，或者比另一种偏爱一道菜的问题，还在于发明新的食物。在人类的实际生活中发生了如此多的机械和科学变化，以至于可以实现烹饪的完美并组织各种口味，气味和功能，直到昨天，由于存在的一般条件，这些东西似乎是荒谬的也不同。我们必须通过不断改变食物的种类及其组合，消除古老的，根深蒂固的味觉习惯，为未来的化学食品做准备。我们甚至可以为人类做好准备，以通过无线电广播滋养波的可能性不太遥远。
 未来主义与法西斯主义的联系不是轻描淡写的事情，但同样重要的是要记住，马里内蒂（Marinetti）认为，艺术家有责任提出一个大胆的公众人物形象。他过着轻柔的羽毛。 
任务完成。他的反面声明引起了当地和美国民众的愤怒。
 那不勒斯市市长波维诺公爵对马里内蒂的说法做出了回应，他说：“意大利面完全反对那不勒斯人的活泼精神和热情，慷慨，直觉的灵魂”，他说：“天堂里的天使只吃细面条。玛琳蒂（Marinetti）轻率地证明了“天堂和天使生命的令人垂涎的单调”。
 他为一个充满未来感的世界而鼓动，在这个世界中，厨房将装有“大气和真空蒸馏器，离心高压灭菌器（和）透析器”。
 特雷弗·邓塞思（Trevor Dunseith）发现，他的食谱作为一次性表演艺术，其功能比去碗碟中添加更多的烹饪美食要好。
 原因是朱莉娅·希尔德（Julia Child）的Coq a Vin和Tarte Tatin忍受，而马里蒂（Marinetti）的“兴奋的猪”和“黑衬衫小吃”却被废弃了。
   马里内蒂在支持法西斯主义的程度上也支持法西斯主义，但他的忠诚最终动摇了。对于马里内蒂（Marinetti）而言，罗马废墟和文艺复兴时期的绘画不仅无聊，而且与进步相反。相比之下，对墨索里尼而言，它们在政治上是有用的。这位独裁者借鉴了意大利的历史，以寻求建立一个新的强大的国家-这也导致了一场全国食品自给自足运动，鼓励人们种植和消费小麦，大米和葡萄等传统食品。政府甚至资助了小麦营养价值的研究，一位科学家声称全麦面包可以提高生育能力。简而言之，战前的未来主义梦想再次被提出。
       阿云·哈里代（Ayun Halliday）是东村因基（East Village Inky）杂志的作者，插画家，戏剧制作人和首席灵长类动物学家。在格雷格·科蒂斯（Greg Kotis）的短片《 L'Ourse》中，将她看作加拿大加拿大人的熊，前往纽约寻找食物和寓意。跟随她@AyunHalliday。
note_en:
We must fight against puddles of sauce, disordered heaps of food, and above all, against flabby, anti-virile pastasciutta. —poet Filippo Tommaso Marinetti
 Odds are  Filippo Tommaso Marinetti, the father of Futurism and a dedicated provocateur, would be crestfallen to discover how closely his most incendiary gastronomical pronouncement aligns with the views of today’s low-carb crusaders.
 In denouncing pasta, “that absurd Italian gastronomic religion,” his intention was to shock and criticize the bourgeoisie, not  reduce bloat and inflammation.
 He did, however, share the popular 21st-century view that heavy pasta meals leave diners feeling equally heavy and lethargic.
   Futurist cooking will be free of the old obsessions with volume and weight and will have as one of its principles the abolition of pastasciutta. Pastasciutta, however agreeable to the palate, is a passéist food because it makes people heavy, brutish, deludes them into thinking it is nutritious, makes them skeptical, slow, pessimistic… Any pastascuittist who honestly examines his conscience at the moment he ingurgitates his biquotidian pyramid of pasta will find within the gloomy satisfaction of stopping up a black hole. This voracious hole is an incurable sadness of his. He may delude himself, but nothing can fill it. Only a Futurist meal can lift his spirits. And pasta is anti-virile because a heavy, bloated stomach does not encourage physical enthusiasm for a woman, nor favour the possibility of possessing her at any time.
  Bombast came naturally to him. While he truly believed in the tenets of  Futurism —speed, industry, technology, and the cleansing effects of war, at the expense of tradition and the past —he gloried in hyperbole, absurdity, and showy pranks.
 The Futurist Cookbook  reflects this, although it does contain actual recipes, with very specific instructions as to how each dish should be served. A sample:
 RAW MEAT TORN BY TRUMPET BLASTS: cut a perfect cube of beef. Pass an electric current through it, then marinate it for twenty-four hours in a mixture of rum, cognac and white vermouth. Remove it from the mixture and serve on a bed of red pepper, black pepper and snow. Each mouthful is to be chewed carefully for one minute, and each mouthful is divided from the next by vehement blasts on the trumpet blown by the eater himself.
 Intrepid host Trevor Dunseith documents his attempt to stage a faithful Futurist dinner party in the above video.
 Guests eat salad with their hands for maximum “pre-labial tactile pleasure” before balancing oranges stuffed with antipasto on their heads to randomize the selection of each mouthful. While not all of the flavors were a hit, the party agreed that the experience was —as intended —totally novel (and 100% pasta free).
 Marinetti’s anti-pasta campaign chimed with Prime Minister Benito Mussolini’s goal of eliminating Italy’s economic dependence on foreign markets — the Battle for Grain. Northern farmers could produce ample supplies of rice, but nowhere near the amount of wheat needed to support the populace’s pasta consumption. If Italians couldn’t grow more wheat, Mussolini wanted them to shift from pasta to rice.
   Marinetti agreed that rice would be the “patriotic” choice, but his desired ends were rooted in his own avant-garde art movement:
 … it is not just a question of replacing pasta with rice, or of preferring one dish to another, but of inventing new foods. So many mechanical and scientific changes have come into effect in the practical life of mankind that it is also possible to achieve culinary perfection and to organize various tastes, smells and functions, something which until yesterday would have seemed absurd because the general conditions of existence were also different. We must, by continually varying types of food and their combinations, kill off the old, deeply rooted habits of the palate, and prepare men for future chemical foodstuffs. We may even prepare mankind for the not-too-distant possibility of broadcasting nourishing waves over the radio.
 Futurism’s  ties to fascism are not a thing to brush off lightly, but it’s also important to remember that Marinetti believed it was the artist’s duty to put forward a bold public personae. He lived to ruffle feathers.
  Mission accomplished. His anti-pasta pronouncements resulted in a tumult of public indignation, both locally and in the States.
 The Duke of Bovino, mayor of Naples, reacted to Marinetti’s statement that pasta is “completely hostile to the vivacious spirit and passionate, generous, intuitive soul of the Neapolitans” by saying, “The angels in Heaven eat nothing but  vermicelli al pomodoro.” Proof, Marinetti sniped back, of “the unappetizing monotony of Paradise and of the life of the Angels.”
 He agitated for a futuristic world in which kitchens would be stocked with ”atmospheric and vacuum stills, centrifugal autoclaves (and) dialyzers.”
 His recipes, as Trevor Dunseith discovered, function better as one-time performance art than go-to dishes to add to one’s culinary repertoire.
 There is a reason why  Julia Child’s Coq a Vin and Tarte Tatin endure while Marinetti’s   Excited Pig and Black Shirt Snack have fallen into disuse.
   Marinetti supported Fascism to the extent that it too advocated progress, but his allegiance eventually wavered. To Marinetti, Roman ruins and Renaissance paintings were not only boring but also antithetical to progress. To Mussolini, by contrast, they were politically useful. The dictator drew on Italian history in his quest to build a new, powerful nation—which also led to a national campaign in food self-sufficiency, encouraging the growing and consumption of such traditional foods as wheat, rice, and grapes. The government even funded research into the nutritional benefits of wheat, with one scientist claiming whole-wheat bread boosted fertility. In short, the prewar dream of futurist food was tabled yet again.
       Ayun Halliday is an author, illustrator, theater maker and Chief Primatologist of  the East Village Inky zine. See her as a French Canadian bear who travels to New York City in search of food and meaning in Greg Kotis’ short film,  L’Ourse.  Follow her  @AyunHalliday.
title: 当意大利未来主义者对面食宣战时（1930年）
title_en: When Italian Futurists Declared War on Pasta (1930)

想象一下，您在罗马开了辆出租汽车，而手机上的卫星导航（或GPS）停止工作了。这发生在我两年前，当时我每天从Trastevere的Airbnb乘汽车上下班到Via Amsterdam的办公室。
 在那一刻，我记得在想两件事：我希望我对以前的驱动器上的地标给予更多的关注，并且空间数据应用程序需要高度可用。
 快进到今天，世界就不同了。我们正处于一场全球大流行之中，这场大流行给我们造成了很大的痛苦，并在很大程度上使我们无法自拔。在过去八个月的家中，我发现自己渴望出门旅行。由于这不会在现实中发生，因此我选择了一次假想的旅行，以访问我过去喜欢的一些地方，而这样做的载体是CockroachDB v20.2中新的空间数据功能的简单演示。 。
  高度可用的空间数据演示是一个单页Web应用程序，该应用程序在页面加载时会在地图上的“游客”中显示，该位置是从一小部分中随机选择的，周围是最近的酒吧，咖啡馆，餐馆或酒吧（便利设施的类型也是随机选择的）。在地图上平移时，附近的设施也会更新。单击其中任何一个时，都会显示其名称，以及距游客的距离（以米为单位）。该空间数据演示的组成部分包括：Leaflet，Python，Flask，Mapbox，CockroachDB K8s运算符以及OpenStreetMap中的数据。
 由于CockroachDB提供了高度可用的地图数据，因此无需担心像两年前我的地图应用程序停止工作时滞留我的方式滞留游客。
 下面的视频更详细地描述了所有这些内容，并且该演示的GitHub存储库包含详细的自述文件以及再现该演示所需的一切（除了基础结构和数据，尽管提供了指向数据集的链接）。希望您喜欢这个视频！
note_en:
Imagine you’re driving a rental car in Rome and the satnav (or GPS) on your phone stops working. This happened to me two years ago when I was commuting by car each day from  an Airbnb in Trastevere to  an office on Via Amsterdam.
 In that moment I remember thinking two things: I wish I had paid more attention to landmarks on previous drives and spatial data applications need to be highly available.
 Fast forward to today and the world is different. We’re in the midst of a global pandemic that has inflicted much suffering and has kept us, for the most part, at home. After being home for the past eight months, I find myself longing to get out and travel. Since that’s not going to happen in reality, I’ve opted to take an imaginary trip to visit some places I’ve enjoyed in the past, and the vehicle for this is a  simple demo of the new spatial data functionality in  CockroachDB v20.2.
  The highly available spatial data demo is a single page web app which, when the page loads, shows a “tourist” on a map in a location selected randomly from a small set, surrounded by the nearest pubs, cafes, restaurants, or bars (the type of amenity is also randomly selected). As you pan around the map, the nearby amenities are updated. When any of these is clicked, its name is displayed, along with its distance, in meters, from where the tourist is. The ingredients for this spatial data demo are:  Leaflet, Python,  Flask,  Mapbox, the  CockroachDB K8s operator, and data from  OpenStreetMap.
 Because CockroachDB provides highly available map data, there’s no need to worry about stranding the tourist the way that I was stranded 2 years ago when my map application stopped working.
 The video below describes all of this in more detail, and the demo’s  GitHub repository contains a detailed README along with everything necessary to reproduce the demo (except for infrastructure and data, though a link is provided to the data set).  I hope you enjoy  the video!
title: 高度可用的空间数据：在伦敦寻找酒吧
title_en: Highly Available Spatial Data: Finding Pubs in London

在2018年秋天，我们注意到有一些使Google与其他大型科技公司区分开的地方。每个人都在谈论如何规范这些科技公司，并且每个人都有很好的计划来做，除了Google之外。我们认为，这是因为似乎没有人真正了解Google的工作或工作方式。因此，过去两年来，我们一直在研究和分析Google。通过这项工作，我们发现了一些真正令人信服的证据，证明了Google垄断的力量是什么，以至于美国国会将这项工作作为其最近发布的《大技术反托拉斯报告》的一部分。我们的主要想法是，爬网是自然的垄断，谷歌可以控制这种垄断，一旦您了解了为什么这是真的，这意味着应该采取什么样的法规就变得很清楚了。统治Google在互联网上的力量。
 我们已在该网站上发布了我们认为有助于人们了解Google的基础以及如何规范它们的经济观点。到目前为止，我们已经私下完成了所有这些工作，并逐一与监管者接触并会面。随着《纽约时报》发表有关这项工作的文章，我们正在发布这项研究，以供所有人阅读并开放该俱乐部，供人们加入并参与对Google垄断的研究。明年我们有一个坚实的计划，关于如何完成这项研究，并与立法者和政府合作，以起草并引入法律来规范Google的网络抓取优势。如果您发现自己同意在本网站上阅读的内容，并想像一个更美好的未来，那就是互联网不仅仅是五家大型科技公司相互之间达成交易，请考虑加入The Knuckleheads'Club，并帮助我们赢得更美好的世界。
 因此，在上述介绍的基础上，让我们深入了解Google到底是什么。
note_en:
In the fall of 2018, we noticed that there was something that set Google apart from other big tech companies. Everybody was talking about how to regulate these tech companies, and everybody had pretty good plans for how to do it — for every company besides Google. In our opinion, it was because nobody really seemed to understand what Google does, or how they do it. So, for the past two years, we’ve been researching and analyzing Google. Through this work, we’ve found some really compelling evidence about what powers Google’s monopoly, so much so that  Congress referenced this work as part of their recently released Big Tech Antitrust Report. The big ideas that we have is that that crawling the web is a natural monopoly, that Google has control of that monopoly, and that once you understand why this is true and what it means it becomes pretty clear about what sorts of regulations should be taken to reign in Google’s power over the internet.
 We have published on this website the economic arguments that we have developed that we think help people understand the foundations of Google and how to regulate  them. Up until now we have done all this work in private, reaching out to and meeting with regulators on an individual basis. With the publishing of an article about this work in  The New York Times, we are publishing this research for everyone to read and opening up the club for people to join and participate in researching Google’s monopoly. We have a  solid plan for the next year about how to finish this research and work with legislators and governments to get legislation drafted and introduced to regulate Google’s web crawling advantages. If you find yourself agreeing with what you read on this website and get caught up imagining a better future where the internet isn’t just five big tech companies all cutting deals with each other, please consider  joining the Knuckleheads’ Club and helping us fight to win a better world.
 So, with all that said as an introduction, let’s dive into what Google is exactly.
title: Google在robots.txt中获得优先权限
title_en: Google gets preferential permissions in robots.txt

最近，腾讯投资了FJ Dynamics，这是一家中国初创公司，向全球客户销售农业自动化解决方案，例如智能拖拉机和水稻插秧机以及用于港口和工厂的无人驾驶车辆。腾讯在周二的公告中表示，来自腾讯和其他未公开投资者的资金总计为“数亿人民币”（合1美元= 6.55元人民币）。
 FJ Dynamics成立于2017年，与中国的多家知名公司建立了联系。根据该公司的商业登记文件，其股东包括无人机制造商大疆创新和中国国有汽车制造商东风资产管理。它的名字也有线索：FJ的中文缩写为Feng和Jiang，分别是来自Dongfeng和DJI的中文拼写。该公司的创始人兼首席执行官吴迪也来自大疆创新，他在大疆工作，担任首席科学家，领导芯片研发。
  FJD经济实惠且批量生产的农业机器人。这不是一个昂贵的演示。他们支持地球上农民的可持续经营。 FJD智能农业机械系列包括拖拉机，插秧机，收割机等。了解更多：https://t.co/XvMZpBFI7G #AgriRobot #Smartfarm #FJD
 有趣的是，DJI也在最近大力推动农业无人机本身。
 腾讯投资部执行合伙人杰弗里·李（Jeffrey Li）在4月的一次演讲中说：“我们的社会正在转向提高生产力，这为企业服务带来了增长机会。” “在美国，大量风险投资和私募股权资金流向了以企业为中心的公司；但是在中国，与面向消费者的公司相比，以企业为中心的企业只占一小部分交易。工业的枢纽需要时间。”
 FJ Dynamics在中国注册，声称在中国，瑞典和荷兰设有研发中心。
note_en:
Most recently, Tencent invested in FJ Dynamics, a Chinese startup selling  agricultural automation solutions such as smart tractors and rice transplanters as well as unmanned vehicles for ports and factories to clients around the world. The funding from Tencent and other undisclosed investors amounted  to “hundreds of millions of yuan” ($1= 6.55 yuan), Tencent said in an announcement  on Tuesday.
 Founded 2017, FJ Dynamics has links to several big-name corporations in China. Among its shareholders are drone maker DJI and Dongfeng Asset Management, a Chinese state-owned automaker, according to the firm’s  business registration filing. There are clues in its name, too: FJ is short for “Feng” and “Jiang” in Chinese, which are taken from the Chinese spelling of Dongfeng and DJI respectively. Its founder and chief executive officer Wu Di also hailed from DJI, where he worked as  the chief scientist and led research and development in chips.
  FJD Affordable and Mass-produced Agri-robots. It is not an expensive demo. They support sustainable business for farmers on earth. The series of FJD smart agri-machines includes tractor, transplanter, harvester,etc.. Learn more:  https://t.co/XvMZpBFI7G  #AgriRobot  #Smartfarm  #FJD
 Interestingly, DJI has also been making a strong push into  agricultural drones itself in recent times.
 “Our society is turning to improve productivity, which ushers in growth opportunities for enterprise services,”  said Jeffrey Li, managing partner of Tencent’s investment unit, at a speech in April. “In the U.S., a considerable amount of venture capital and private equity fundings go towards enterprise-focused companies; but in China, enterprised-focused businesses take up only a small share of deals compared to consumer-facing companies. The pivot to industries takes time.”
 Registered in China, FJ Dynamics  claims to have R&D centers in China, Sweden, and the Netherlands.
title: 腾讯投资DJI支持的农业科技初创公司FJ Dynamics
title_en: Tencent invests in DJI-backed agritech startup FJ Dynamics

Zenoti创始人兼首席执行官Sudheer Koneru在接受TechCrunch采访时表示，总部位于华盛顿贝尔维尤的SaaS初创公司周二表示，已在D轮融资中筹集了1.6亿美元，对公司的估值“远远超过10亿美元”。本星期。该轮融资由Advent International牵头，Tiger Global和Steadview Partners参与了此次融资。到目前为止，这家初创公司已经筹集了约251美元。
 Zenoti从印度开始，已经为健康和保健行业构建了云管理解决方案。该创业公司的平台允许客户在约会后直接通过移动应用进行付款。当他们走进商店时，它还会签入并通知提供商有关客户的信息。
  对于他们的客户，Zenoti允许他们接受预订，接受数字付款，处理工资单，管理后端库存，以及将小费从客户直接转移到员工的银行帐户。这家初创公司成立于2010年，但直到2012年它就建立了完整的堆栈并推向市场。
 Zenoti的平台结合了ERP和CRM工具。 Accel合伙人Shekhar Kirani在接受TechCrunch采访时解释说，这就是Zenoti进入之前这个行业服务不足的令人惊讶的地方。
 Accel是第一个支持Zenoti的投资者。他解释说，与像Microsoft或Notion这样的公司提供的服务已经建立了跨行业的“水平”服务不同，spa和沙龙行业需要一个“垂直的”参与者来解决他们所面临的问题，而Microsoft或Notion却提供了这些服务。 Zenoti就是这样做的。
 早就很清楚，Zenoti可以在印度以外的地区寻求客户。事实证明，这种早期投资对初创公司是正确的，美国目前占该公司收入的60％，其次是英国。基拉尼（Kirani）将Zenoti成功地描述为印度的SaaS运动，该运动已经产生了许多初创企业，例如Freshworks，Zoho，MindTickle和Crazybee，这些初创企业起源于全球第二大互联网市场，但如今其大多数客户都在国外。
 随着大流行将企业数字化的多个方面转移，Zenoti的产品在最近几个月被证明更加有用。此后，以前不依赖数字产品的水疗和沙龙连锁店开始使用移动应用程序和减少对纸币的依赖的方法。尽管大流行，该创业公司今年仍增长了100％，是迄今为止最好的。 
最重要的是，Zenoti员工捐赠了25万美元，以帮助他们所服务行业的工人。
note_en:
The Bellevue, Washington-headquartered SaaS startup said on Tuesday it has raised $160 million in its Series D financing round that valued it at “well past $1 billion,” said Sudheer Koneru, founder and chief executive of Zenoti, in an interview with TechCrunch earlier this week. The round was led by  Advent International   with participation from Tiger Global and Steadview Partners. The startup has raised about $251 to date.
 Zenoti, which started its journey in India, has built a cloud management solution for health and wellness industries. The startup’s platform allows customers to pay directly from a mobile app after their appointments. It also checks them in when they walk into the store and notifies the providers about the customer.
  For their clients, Zenoti allows them to accept bookings, accept digital payments, handle payroll, manage backend inventory, and transfer the tip from customers to directly a staff’s bank account. The startup was founded in 2010, but it wasn’t until 2012 when it had built this complete stack and went to the market.
 Zenoti’s platform combines both the ERP and CRM tools. And that’s what this industry, which had surprisingly been underserved prior to Zenoti’s entry, needed, explained Shekhar Kirani, partner at  Accel,   in an interview with TechCrunch.
 Accel was the first investor to back Zenoti. Unlike offerings from companies such as Microsoft or Notion, that have built “horizontal” services that people across the industries use, the spa and salon industry needed a “vertical” player that just looked at solving the issues that they were facing, he explained. Zenoti did just that.
 It became very clear early on that Zenoti could pursue customers beyond India. That early bet has proven right for the startup, for which the U.S. now accounts for 60% of the revenue, followed by the UK. Kirani described the success of Zenoti as a SaaS movement in India, which has produced scores of startups such as Freshworks, Zoho, MindTickle, and Crazybee that started in the world’s second largest internet market but now have most of their customers outside of the country.
 Zenoti’s offerings have proven more useful in recent months as the pandemic moves several aspects of businesses digital. Spa and salon chains that were previously not relying on digital offerings have since embraced mobile apps and ways to cut reliance on paper cash. Despite the pandemic, the startup has grown 100% this year, its best to date.
 On top of that, Zenoti employees donated $250,000 to help the workers in the industry they serve, said Koneru.
title: Zenoti成为独角兽公司，获得1.6亿美元融资
title_en: Zenoti becomes a unicorn with $160 million funding round

 但是现在，生物技术行业表示已经提出了一种解决方案-一种合成的替代方案，该方案不需要燃烧或清除任何雨林。它说，这最终可以取代天然棕榈油，从香波，肥皂，洗涤剂和口红到包装面包，饼干，人造黄油，冰淇淋和巧克力等食品。

  在每公顷产量方面，它还与丰产的作物竞争。

 ＆＃34;但是在洗发水或其他美容产品中，您可能可以竞争，因为价格不是主要动力，＆＃34;查克教授说。

她说，与此同时，该公司专注于通过其天然油棕树的新变种提高每公顷产量，以此来限制转换为棕榈油种植园所需的土地。  但是她也警告说，如果合成棕榈油成为商业上可行的替代品，将会带来无法预料的后果。 ＆＃34;您在印尼的450万农民中仍然会有一些人，他们现在正在种植棕榈油，并且可能会被转移到土地更饿的农作物中，例如橡胶或木材，&& ＃34; 她说。
note_en:
There's an ugly truth to the beauty products we slap on our faces and an unsavoury truth to the foods we eat: Many are made with palm oil, which is responsible for the rapid deforestation of some of the world's most biodiverse forests, destroying the habitat of already endangered species like the orangutan, pygmy elephant and Sumatran rhino.
 But now the biotech industry says it has come up with a solution - a synthetic alternative that doesn't involve burning down or clearing any rainforest. It says this could eventually replace natural palm oil in everything from shampoos, soaps, detergents and lipsticks, to food products like packaged bread, biscuits, margarine, ice cream and chocolate.
 "Over the last 30 years, 50% of palm oil plantation growth has come at the hands of deforestation of tropical forest and peatland," says Shara Ticku, founder of C16 Biosciences, one of the biotech firms pioneering a synthetic alternative. "That is really the core of the problem we're trying to solve."
 Research is still in a pre-commercial stage, but there's been high-profile interest in its potential. Earlier this year, C16 Biosciences, a three-year-old start-up based in New York, received a $20m (£15m) investment from Breakthrough Energy Ventures, a fund backed by Bill Gates and the likes of Amazon's Jeff Bezos, Michael Bloomberg and Virgin's Richard Branson.
 C16 Biosciences is not the only organisation looking to come up with a synthetic alternative. Researchers are working on something similar at the UK's University of Bath and at California-based start-up Kiverdi.
 "GM (genetic modification) engineering has opened up new advances," says Chris Chuck, professor of bioprocess engineering at Bath.
   What these projects have in common is that they use a fermentation process, employing big vats in a procedure similar to brewing beer. At C16 Biosciences this involves using genetically-engineered microbes to convert food waste and industrial by-products into a product that is chemically very similar to natural palm oil.
 "It's a yeast, we feed it sugars, then the yeast grows and they're able to produce large amounts of oil within their cells, and we have to squeeze out that oil or extract it," Ms Ticku says.
 At the moment, the focus of C16 Biosciences is on creating a prototype, and getting feedback from companies that might choose to use it in their products. It has already had expressions of interest from Germany-based international food wholesalers, Metro Group.
  "I think we can definitely imagine this (as a selling point) especially in non-food products, explaining to the customer that there is a synthetic palm oil used as an ingredient in, for example, dishwashing liquid and I think that customers will accept it," says Veronika Pountcheva, Metro's director of corporate responsibility.
 But the challenges are substantial. To succeed commercially and at scale, a synthetic alternative must be capable of mimicking natural palm oil's versatility, making it a suitable replacement in everything from food to household products.
 Natural palm oil has a smooth and creamy texture, and is odourless, making it a useful ingredient in many recipes. It is semi-solid at room temperature, so it can keep things like margarine spreadable, and it has a natural preservative effect that extends the shelf life of food products.
 "Ultimately these (challenges) have technological solutions, it can be technically done," says Prof Chuck. "The real problem is cost, because natural palm oil is extremely cheap, and that's what a synthetic alternative is competing against."
  It is also competing against a crop that is hugely productive in terms of volume produced per hectare.
 Prof Chuck's team calculate that synthetic palm oil is between two to three times more expensive than its natural version, and that's in a best-case, most cost-effective scenario. "In uses where price is important and is the main driver, for example in biofuel and food, that means a synthetic alternative is going to struggle."
 Added to that is the aversion of consumers in many markets to eating anything that might contain an ingredient derived from genetically modified microbes.
 "But in shampoo or other beauty products, you might be able to compete, because price isn't the main driver," says Prof Chuck.
 That means a synthetic alternative risks becoming a niche product. The World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) says that globally, 70% of the 75 million tonnes of palm oil consumed annually is used as cooking oil and as a food ingredient.
 It estimates global consumption will rise to between 264 and 447 million tonnes by 2050, with an estimated five-fold increase in demand for palm-based biofuels by 2030. A synthetic alternative then may only make a small dent in natural palm oil's global production.
  That however is not putting off C16 Bioscience's Shara Ticku: "We believe that with our technology platform, at a scale of hundreds of thousands of kilograms annually, we will be cost competitive with palm oil. If we can get enough people to change then there is no longer any justified reason for burning forest to produce this vegetable oil, and that is a success."
 Natural palm oil producers are keeping an eye these developments. "We watch them closely, but I don't think that realistically that alternative is there yet in terms of either its ability to produce at scale or the cost efficiencies," says Anita Neville of Indonesia's Golden Agri-Resources, one of the world's largest privately-owned palm oil plantation companies.
 In the meantime, she says, the firm is focused on improving its yield per hectare with new variants of its natural oil palm trees, as a way of limiting the land needed to convert to palm oil plantations.
 But she warns too of unforeseen consequences if synthetic palm oil becomes a commercially viable alternative. "You're still going to have something in the region of 4.5 million farmers in Indonesia, who are growing palm oil today and who might be moved into crops that are more land hungry, for example rubber or timber," she says.

note: 96％的癌症患者声称自己的健康状况比普通癌症患者好。  当我知道这一点时，它震撼了我的灵魂。 一开始，我几乎和所有人一样，想：“是的，但我确实高于平均水平！” 然后我意识到自己又在做。  所以我决定反其道而行之。 现在我只是假设我低于平均水平。  它对我很好。 我多听。 我问了很多问题。 我不再认为别人很愚蠢。 我认为大多数人都比我聪明。  假设您低于平均水平就是承认您还在学习中。 您专注于需要改进的地方，而不是过去的成就。  许多人非常担心自己看起来不错，以至于从来没有做过任何伟大的事情。 许多人非常担心做一件伟大的事情，以至于根本不做任何事情。  当您认为自己只是一个学生，而您当前的行为只是一种练习时，就会破坏这种瘫痪状态。
note_en:
Ninety-six percent of cancer patients claim to be in better health than the average cancer patient.
    When I learned this, it shook my soul.	At first, like almost everybody, I thought, “Yes, but I really am above average!”	Then I realized I was doing it again.
 So I decided to gamble on the opposite.	Now I just assume I’m below average.
 It serves me well.	I listen more.	I ask a lot of questions.	I’ve stopped thinking  others are stupid.	I assume most people are smarter than me.
 To assume you’re below average is to admit you’re still learning.	You focus on what you need to improve, not your  past accomplishments.
 Many people are so worried about looking good that they never do anything great.	Many people are so worried about doing something great that they never do anything at all.
 You destroy that paralysis when you think of yourself as just a student, and your current actions as just practice.
title: 假设您低于平均水平

note_en: 2020 has seen its fair number of challenges and changes. We’ve all adapted to new ways of staying in touch, getting work done, celebrating birthdays and weddings, and even exercising. When you start or join a group call, Signal will display the participants in a grid view. You can also swipe up to switch to a view that automatically focuses the screen on who is speaking, and it will update in real time as the active speaker changes.  Signal Group Calls are one of many features that we have designed with  Signal Private Groups as a foundation, using our  RingRTC library for handling frame encryption and the logic around setting up and joining calls.  Free group calls are available starting today. Update your apps to the latest versions to get started. Group calls are only supported in new style Signal groups. You won’t see the call button in your legacy groups, but your legacy Signal groups will start automatically updating to New Groups in the coming weeks. Group calls are currently limited to 5 participants, but we’re working to let you have even more participants in a group call soon. We hope group calls are a helpful new way to connect!

危害公共和私人组织的供应链攻击背后的黑客，已经设计出一种巧妙的方法来绕过保护其目标网络的多因素身份验证系统。
  安全公司Volexity的研究人员周一表示，在2019年底和2020年初，他们遇到了同样的攻击者，因为他们深入智囊团组织内部的次数不少于三次。在一次入侵期间，Volexity研究人员注意到黑客使用一种新颖的技术绕过了Duo提供的MFA保护。在受感染的网络上获得管理员特权后，黑客利用这些不受束缚的权利从运行Outlook Web App的服务器中窃取了称为akey的Duo机密，企业使用该机密提供各种网络服务的帐户身份验证。
 然后，黑客使用akey生成cookie，因此，当拥有正确用户名和密码的人接管帐户时，他们会准备好cookie。 Volexity指国家赞助的黑客组织Dark Halo。研究人员Damien Cash，Matthew Meltzer，Sean Koessel，Steven Adair和Thomas Lancaster写道：
 在第二次Volexity涉及Dark Halo的事件即将结束时，观察到该演员通过OWA访问了用户的电子邮件帐户。出于某些原因，这是意外的，其中最重要的原因是目标邮箱受MFA保护。来自Exchange服务器的日志显示，攻击者提供的用户名和密码身份验证正常，但没有通过Duo受到第二方面的挑战。来自Duo身份验证服务器的日志进一步表明，未尝试登录到相关帐户。 Volexity能够确认不涉及会话劫持，并且通过OWA服务器的内存转储，还可以确认攻击者提供了与名为duo-sid的Duo MFA会话绑定的cookie。
 Volexity对这一事件的调查确定，攻击者已从OWA服务器访问了Duo集成密钥（akey）。然后，该密钥使攻击者可以得出在duo-sid cookie中设置的预先计算的值。成功进行密码身份验证后，服务器评估了duo-sid cookie并确定其有效。这使攻击者知道用户帐户和密码，然后完全绕过帐户上设置的MFA。此事件强调了确保与密钥集成关联的所有机密（例如与MFA提供者的机密）在发生泄露后应进行更改的必要性。此外，重要的是，不仅要在违规后更改密码，而且不要将密码设置为与以前的密码类似的内容（例如，Summer2020！vs Spring2020！或SillyGoo $ e3 vs SillyGoo $ e2）。
 Volexity对Dark Halo的描述加强了其他研究人员所做的观察，即黑客是高技能的。 Volexity说，攻击者在智囊团客户认为该组织已被驱逐后反复返回。最终，Volexity说，攻击者“几年来仍未被发现”。
    《华盛顿邮报》和《纽约时报》均援引了政府人士的不愿透露姓名的说法，称黑客入侵背后的组织既被称为APT29，又被称为俄罗斯联邦安全局（FSB）的高级持续威胁组织Cozy Bear。 
尽管本例中的MFA提供者是Duo，但它也很可能涉及其任何竞争对手。 MFA威胁建模通常不包括OWA服务器的完整系统危害。黑客获得的访问级别足以阻止几乎所有防御措施。
  思科公司的Duo Security注意到最近有一个安全研究人员的博客文章，该文章讨论了在过去一年中从特定威胁参与者团体中观察到的多个安全事件。其中一件事件涉及Duo与Outlook Web Application（OWA）的集成。
  而是，该帖子详细介绍了一个攻击者，该攻击者从现有的受感染客户环境（例如电子邮件服务器）中获得了对集成凭据的特权访问，这些集成凭据对于Duo服务的管理是必不可少的。
 为了减少发生此类事件的可能性，至关重要的是保护集成秘密以防组织内暴露，并在怀疑有折中的情况下轮换秘密。与MFA提供程序集成的服务的破坏可能导致集成秘密的泄露以及对MFA保护的系统和数据的潜在访问。
 Volexity说，Dark Halo的主要目标是获取智囊团内部特定个人的电子邮件。这家安全公司表示，Dark Halo是一个复杂的威胁参与者，与任何知名的威胁参与者没有任何联系。
note_en:
The hackers behind the  supply chain attack that compromised public and private organizations have devised a clever way to bypass multi-factor-authentication systems protecting the networks they target.
  Researchers from security firm Volexity  said on Monday that it had encountered the same attackers in late 2019 and early 2020 as they penetrated deep inside of a think tank organization no fewer than three times. During one of the intrusions, Volexity researchers noticed the hackers using a novel technique to bypass MFA protections provided by Duo. After having gained administrator privileges on the infected network, the hackers used those unfettered rights to steal a Duo secret known as an akey from a server running  Outlook Web App, which enterprises use to provide account authentication for various network services.
 The hackers then used the akey to generate a cookie, so they’d have it ready when someone with the right username and password would need when taking over an account. Volexity refers to the state-sponsored hacker group as Dark Halo. Researchers Damien Cash, Matthew Meltzer, Sean Koessel, Steven Adair, and Thomas Lancaster wrote:
 Toward the end of the second incident that Volexity worked involving Dark Halo, the actor was observed accessing the e-mail account of a user via OWA. This was unexpected for a few reasons, not least of which was the targeted mailbox was protected by MFA. Logs from the Exchange server showed that the attacker provided username and password authentication like normal but were not challenged for a second factor through Duo. The logs from the Duo authentication server further showed that no attempts had been made to log into the account in question. Volexity was able to confirm that session hijacking was not involved and, through a memory dump of the OWA server, could also confirm that the attacker had presented cookie tied to a Duo MFA session named duo-sid.
 Volexity’s investigation into this incident determined the attacker had accessed the Duo integration secret key (akey) from the OWA server. This key then allowed the attacker to derive a pre-computed value to be set in the duo-sid cookie. After successful password authentication, the server evaluated the duo-sid cookie and determined it to be valid. This allowed the attacker with knowledge of a user account and password to then completely bypass the MFA set on the account. This event underscores the need to ensure that all secrets associated with key integrations, such as those with an MFA provider, should be changed following a breach. Further, it is important that not only are passwords changed after a breach, but that passwords are not set to something similar to the previous password (e.g., Summer2020! versus Spring2020! or SillyGoo$e3 versus SillyGoo$e2).
 Volexity’s account of Dark Halo reinforces observations other researchers have made that the hackers are highly skilled. Volexity said the attackers returned repeatedly after the think tank client believed the group had been ejected. Ultimately, Volexity said, the attackers were able to “remain undetected for several years.”
    Both The Washington Post and New York Times have cited government people granted anonymity saying the group behind the hacks was known both as APT29 and Cozy Bear, an advanced persistent threat group believed to be part of the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB).
 While the MFA provider in this case was Duo, it just as easily could have involved any of its competitors. MFA threat modeling generally doesn’t include a complete system compromise of an OWA server. The level of access the hacker achieved was enough to neuter just about any defense.
  Duo Security at Cisco is aware of a recent security researcher blog post discussing multiple security incidents observed over the course of the last year from a particular threat actor group. One of those incidents involved Duo's integration for the Outlook Web Application (OWA).
  Rather, the post details an attacker that achieved privileged access to integration credentials, that are integral for the management of the Duo service, from within an existing compromised customer environment, such as an email server.
 In order to reduce the likelihood of such an event, it is critical to protect integration secrets from exposure within an organization and to rotate secrets if compromise is suspected. Compromise of a service that is integrated with an MFA provider can result in disclosure of integration secrets along with potential access to a system and data that MFA protects.
 Volexity said that Dark Halo’s primary goal was obtaining emails of specific individuals inside the think tank. The security company said Dark Halo is a sophisticated threat actor that had no links to any publicly known threat actors.

谢谢死后一个多星期，愤怒情绪在Zappos前首席执行官的故居拉斯维加斯酝酿。
  谢家祥（Tony Hsieh）在康涅狄格州因感恩节前的大火而丧生时濒临死亡，U-Haul卡车和拖车开始抵达犹他州帕克城（Park City），度假城市扎波斯（Zappos）背后的有远见者迅速建立了享乐主义的飞地。无论谢谢死多几天，他工资单上的数十名技术人员，艺术家和其他创意人士都清楚地知道党已经结束了。一位邻居说，它们似乎在一夜之间消失了。
  与谢建华在过去十年中一直试图单枪匹马振兴的拉斯维加斯市中心形成鲜明对比。 Zappos总部前仍然堆满了笔记和鲜花，而他的影像（数百英尺宽）正投射在Fremont Street Experience的巨型LED屏幕上。拉斯维加斯市长卡罗琳·古德曼（Carolyn Goodman）告诉《福布斯》：“这里令人难过。”
 还有一个安静的愤怒，直接针对这个所谓的“ Park City Crew”的成员，其中许多人正慢慢回到拉斯维加斯。 “我认为无论在哪里的人，都看到他在伤害自己，应该做正确的事，”谢家的朋友迈克尔·康思韦特说，已经有十多年了。另一个长期的朋友在他的圈子中描述了两部分的愤怒，“因为，一个人失去了托尼，他们觉得托尼周围没有任何人爱他，他所拥有的只是那些从他身上受益的人。”
 在记录了谢先生的生活，他对维加斯的影响以及今年以来的下滑之后，福布斯与他在最后几个月与谢先生一起生活在犹他州的更多人以及其他几位熟悉发生了什么的老朋友进行了交谈。他们展示了加入谢赫在帕克城的一群人是如何在他的薪水上沉迷于他最坏的行为的，因为他的身心健康状况恶化了。用一位老朋友的话说：“这是操纵性的水ches，他们以最黑暗的方式利用某人的弱点谋取自己的利益。＆＃34;
   谢谢的长期朋友第一次注意到他在2019年最后几个月的谈话中变得更加孤僻，甚至遥远，谢谢的血统几乎恰好与他搬迁至帕克城的时间相吻合。 1月，他参加了在那里的圣丹斯电影节，当时有谣传在拉斯维加斯传开，谢谢一直在与一群年轻的朋友们开派对。
 几天后，谢女士短暂回到拉斯维加斯，主持了两个当地小企业主的婚礼。他似乎与众不同。在活动中拍摄的照片在谢尔盖·科特斯赌场（El Cortez Casino）街对面的一个地点举行，并与《福布斯》（Forbes）分享，显示谢谢穿着一件鲜艳的橙色毛衣，挥舞着长条，上面贴着便条纸。据围观者说，当他带领这对新人走过他们的誓言时，谢似乎似乎很生气，在仪式上抽着骆驼烟。在就座酒会上，当亲朋好友用演讲向新婚夫妇敬酒时，谢和一群人在附近的垫子上做瑜伽。参加婚礼的一位人士说：“很多人都很担心。” 
Hsieh意识到自己的行为越来越热闹，就搬到了他的新家Park City。他告诉人们，他打算专注于健康，保健和康复。参观者引用了帕克市日程中内置的健康方面，从瑜伽到冥想再到骑马，其中一些由谢茜红颜小伙伴苏西·巴列森（Suzie Baleson）共同创立的公司Wellth Collective组织。 （Baleson未回复置评请求）。一位朋友于2月下旬加入谢贤，当时是他在帕克城租房的时候，他说，他从未见过这位技术企业家。
 但是谢贤的撤退很快就变得更加险恶，因为在接下来的几个月里，帕克城的船员加入了他，来到了这座山城。正如《福布斯》先前报道的那样，谢家华为他们中的许多人提供了“双倍薪水”的要约，条件是他们“很幸福”并加入帕克城。这一提议很快就被人们接受。
  为了容纳他不断发展的社区，谢先生逐渐购买了九处房产，价格达到八位数，约占这座古老的湖畔阿斯彭斯普林斯社区的15％，这激怒了居住在那里的前企业高管和银行家。随着岁月的流逝，随着人们开始赚钱并免费居住，衣架的数量增加到了几十个，同时旨在帮助实现谢先生的许多愿景。
 谢建华拥护宏伟的计划，从创造世界和平到在犹他州建立另一个燃烧的人。在大流行期间，他还竭尽全力为帕克城的当地企业提供支持，为团队购买了整个餐厅露台。 Hsieh在Covid快速测试上花了大笔钱，以保持聚会的进行。
 这些“员工”试图通过回应谢震的每一个心血来证明自己的演出是合理的。一位居民回想起一堵墙，谢谢将在上面贴上“赏金”，或者谢谢要为某人完成支付100美元或更多的任务。有些人计划建造一个有趣的公园，例如努力在其中一处建造冰吧。
  但是其他项目则比较不理想。一位便利贴要求提供一份有关一氧化二氮的研究报告，谢谢有时似乎几乎连续消耗一氧化二氮。一位目击者甚至说谢谢在与帕克市市长安迪·比尔曼（Andy Beerman）午餐时拿着一个罐子。 （市长拒绝在会议上发表评论，并补充说谢谢只是在度假城市居住了很短的时间。）谢谢告诉一位居民，他指示他的助手们制作了一部关于笑气的纪录片。
 越来越多的谢谢接受他提出的移居到犹他州的提议，还与新上司一起频繁吸毒。除了一氧化二氮外，还有致幻剂DMT和受欢迎的俱乐部药物氯胺酮-两种高点都会产生类似ance的状态。 
妄想症与吸毒同步增长。在拉斯维加斯，谢辉一生的大部分时间都拥护社区和“专政”的价值观，或者说是一种非等级制的管理哲学，在这种哲学中，没有一个人比另一个人拥有更高的职位。几年来，他与朋友一起住在Airstream拖车中。但是在帕克城，保安人员封锁了主屋的各个部分，封锁了没有谢谢准许进入的任何人。
 到了夏天，谢恩的随行人员已经将他们在阿斯彭斯普林斯（Aspen Springs）社区的部分变成了一个掩体。在谢女士面前，这是在街上停着一辆不知名的汽车可以促使警方检查的地方。但是谢谢的物业前停着数十辆汽车。社区委员会主任给福布斯的一封电子邮件显示，安全的方阵是“令人恐惧的，尤其是对于现在拒绝在该物业旁行走的儿童而言”。
  回到拉斯维加斯，长期的朋友越来越担心自己无法联系谢。一些人参观了这座城市，看看发生了什么事，还有一些人没有得到观众的支持就离开了。一位朋友说，在拉斯维加斯，“这是开放政策。” “但是在帕克城，可能要花一个半小时甚至到门外。即使那样，您可能仍然看不到他。”
 这些老朋友看到了帕克城船员的年轻，默默无闻的成员在谢家周围围栏。 “所有这些新面孔都出现了，”一位八月份的老朋友说。 “然而，您正在保护他的隐私以破坏自己。”到感恩节，这已被证明是预言。
 谢贤过世后，其父亲理查德·谢谢（Richard Hsieh）和哥哥安德鲁·谢谢（Andrew Hsieh）被任命为其遗产的特别管理人员，据估计，该遗产拥有超过10亿美元的资产。这将是一项艰巨的任务。虽然大多数帕克城乘员迅速离开，但仍有少数人保留。
 拉斯维加斯的一位老朋友说：“可悲的是，很多人仍然住在他们死去的朋友付钱的房屋中。”
  我是《福布斯》的撰稿人。在Twitter上关注我，或通过aau-yeung@forbes.com向我发送电子邮件。

Mike McGregor/Getty Images More than a week after Hsieh died, anger is brewing in Las Vegas, the adopted home of the former Zappos CEO.
  As Tony Hsieh lay near death in Connecticut, felled by a pre-Thanksgiving fire, U-Haul trucks and trailers began arriving in Park City, Utah, the resort city where the visionary behind Zappos had quickly constructed a hedonistic enclave. No matter that it would be several more days before Hsieh died — the several dozen technologists, artists and other creatives on his payroll clearly understood that the party was over. According to a neighbor, they appeared to disappear overnight.
  Contrast that with the scene in downtown Las Vegas, which Hsieh had spent the previous decade trying to almost single-handedly revitalize. Notes and flowers still pile up in front of the Zappos headquarters, while his image, hundreds of feet wide, is being projected across the mammoth LED screens of the Fremont Street Experience. “There’s a sadness here,” Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman tells  Forbes.
 There’s also a quiet fury, aimed directly at members of this so-called “Park City Crew,” many of whom are slowly filtering back to Las Vegas. “I think whoever was there, they saw him hurting himself, they should have done the right thing,” says Michael Cornthwaite, a friend of Hsieh’s for more than a decade. Another longtime friend describes a two-part anger among his circle, “because, one, they lost Tony, and they felt Tony didn’t have anyone who loved him around him, all he had was people who financially benefited from him.”
 After  documenting Hsieh’s life, the impact he had on Vegas and his downward spiral this year,  Forbes spoke with more people who were living with Hsieh in Utah over his final months, and several other older friends familiar with what happened. They show how this group of people who joined Hsieh in Park City indulged in his worst behaviors while on his payroll, as his mental and physical health deteriorated. In the words of one old friend: “This is about manipulative leeches who exploited someone’s weakness for their own gain in the darkest of all possible ways."
   While longtime friends of Hsieh’s first noticed him becoming more withdrawn, even distant, during conversations in the last months of 2019, Hsieh’s descent coincided almost exactly with his relocation to Park City. In January, he attended the Sundance Film Festival there, as rumors swirled around Las Vegas that Hsieh had been partying hard with a new group of younger friends.
 A few days later, Hsieh returned briefly to Las Vegas to officiate the wedding of two local small business owners. He seemed different. Photos taken at the event, held at a venue across the street from the El Cortez Casino, and shared with  Forbes, show Hsieh wearing a bright orange sweater and brandishing a long stick covered in post-it notes. As he walked the couple through their vows, Hsieh seemed inebriated, according to onlookers, chain-smoking Camel cigarettes through the ceremony. During the seated reception, as friends and family toasted the newlyweds with speeches, Hsieh and a group of people did yoga on mats nearby. “A lot of people were concerned,” says one person who attended the wedding.
  Realizing the growing buzz around his behavior, Hsieh relocated to his new home, Park City. He told people he intended to focus on health, wellness and rehabilitation. Visitors cite aspects of wellness built in to his Park City schedule — from yoga to meditation to horseback riding, some of which was organized by Wellth Collective, a company cofounded by Hsieh confidante Suzie Baleson. (Baleson did not respond to requests for comment). A friend who joined Hsieh in Park City in late February, when he was renting a house at the time, said he had never seen the tech entrepreneur in better shape.
 But Hsieh’s retreat soon descended into something more sinister as the Park City Crew joined him in the mountain city over the following months. As  Forbes previously reported, Hsieh made many of them a ‘double-your-best-salary’ offer to join him in Park City on the condition they ‘be happy’ — a proposition many quickly accepted.
  To house his growing community, Hsieh gradually purchased nine properties, at a cost running into eight digits, amounting to about 15% of the ritzy, lakeside Aspen Springs neighborhood, riling the former corporate executives and bankers who lived there. As the year wore on, the number of hangers-on rose into the dozens as people came to make cash and stay for free, while purporting to help realize Hsieh’s many visions.
 Hsieh espoused grand plans, from creating world peace to building another Burning Man in Utah. He also went out of his way to support local businesses in Park City amid the pandemic, buying out entire restaurant patios for his group. Hsieh spent big money on Covid rapid tests to keep the party going.
 These “employees” tried to justify their gigs by answering to Hsieh’s every whim. One resident recalls a wall of post-its, on which Hsieh would post “bounties” — or tasks that Hsieh would then pay $100 or more for someone to complete. Some were in line with building a fun park, such as an effort to construct an ice bar at one of the properties.
  But other projects were more untoward. One post-it asked for a research report on nitrous oxide, which Hsieh seemed to be consuming almost continuously at times — one witness even says Hsieh was holding a canister while lunching with Park City’s mayor, Andy Beerman. (The mayor declined to comment on the meeting, adding that Hsieh resided in the resort city for only a short period.) At one point, Hsieh told a resident that he had directed some of his acolytes to create a documentary on the laughing gas.
 Increasingly, many who took Hsieh up on his offer to move to Utah also took part in frequent drug use alongside their new boss. Besides nitrous oxide, there was also the hallucinogen DMT and the popular club drug ketamine — both the kinds of highs that generate a trance-like state.
  Paranoia increased in lockstep with the drug use. In Las Vegas, Hsieh, who had spent most of his life espousing values of communities and “holocracy,” or a non-hierarchical management philosophy where no individual was more senior-ranking than another, was famously approachable. For several years, he lived among friends in an Airstream trailer. But in Park City, security guards barricaded sections of the main house, blocking anyone who didn’t have Hsieh’s permission to enter.
 By the summer, Hsieh’s entourage had transformed their section of the Aspen Springs community into something of a bunker. Before Hsieh, it was the kind of place where an unknown car parked in the street could prompt a police inspection. But dozens of cars were parked in front of Hsieh’s properties. And the phalanx of security was “intimidating, especially to children that now refuse to walk by the property,” according to an email sent from the director of the neighborhood board to residents, shared with  Forbes.
  Back in Las Vegas, longtime friends were becoming increasingly concerned by their inability to contact Hsieh. Some visited the city to see what was going on — and some left without being granted an audience. In Las Vegas, “it was an open-door policy, period,” a friend said. “But in Park City, it might take you over an hour and a half to even get to the door. And even then you might not see him.”
 These old friends saw the younger, unknown members of the Park City Crew put a barrier around Hsieh. “There were all these new faces,” says one longtime friend who visited in August. “And yet you’re protecting his privacy to just destroy himself.” By Thanksgiving, that had proven prophetic.
 After his passing, Hsieh’s father, Richard Hsieh, and brother, Andrew Hsieh, were made the special administrators to his estate, which is estimated to hold more than half a billion dollars in assets. It’s going to be a large undertaking. While most of the Park City Crew left quickly, a handful have remained.
 “The sad part,” says an old Las Vegas friend, “is a lot of these people are still living in these houses paid for by their dead friend.”
  I am a staff writer at Forbes. Follow me on Twitter or send me an email at aau-yeung@forbes.com.
title: 谢天华（Tony Hsieh）在维加斯（Vegas）举行纪念活动，手指指向“ Park City Crew”
title_en: As Tony Hsieh is memorialized in Vegas, fingers point at the 'Park City Crew'

note:
它们是基因编辑革命的标榜动物，一个又一个地出现。一家美国初创公司通过向奶牛的基因组中添加几封DNA字母，设计出一种方法来确保这些动物永远不会长出麻烦的角。
 对于Recombinetics公司（制造无角牛的明尼苏达州圣保罗市基因编辑公司）而言，这些动物是更好，更快，分子农业新时代的使者。该公司当时的首席执行官塔米·李·史坦诺奇（Tammy Lee Stanoch）在2017年宣称：“通过在农场进行育种，也可以实现同样的结果。这就是精确育种。”
  美国食品药品监督管理局的科学家仔细研究了其中一只名为布里的公牛的基因组序列，发现其基因组中含有一段细菌DNA，其中包括赋予抗生素抗性的基因。
 政府说，来自不同物种的DNA的“意外”添加是在基因编辑过程本身发生的。即使该公司吹捧该动物为100％牛并被FDA指控完全需要对动物进行监管，该公司仍未发现它。
 拥有动物的Recombinetics子公司Acceligen的首席执行官Tad Sontesgard说：“这不是我们期望的，我们也没有寻找它。”他说“应该做一个更完整的检查”。
   这种错误是重组技术的挫折，重组技术的开创性原型包括耐热的牛以及从未触及青春期的猪。这也是对使这种基因编辑成为动物繁殖常规做法的努力的打击。一直以来，Recombinetics一直强烈反对FDA的监督，FDA将基因编辑的动物归为需要大量测试和批准的新药。它甚至游说特朗普政府从卫生局手中夺取监督权，称其正在遏制bar的革命。
 但是基因编辑还没有启动子所说的那样可预测或可靠。取而代之的是，旨在对DNA进行精确更改的过程可能会引起重大的意想不到的更改，而无需任何人注意。由亚历克西斯·诺里斯（Alexis Norris）和希瑟·伦巴第（Heather Lombardi）领导的FDA科学家在7月份发表的论文中写道：“随着基因组编辑技术的发展，我们对它产生的意想不到的改变的理解也随之增加。”他们认为基因编辑错误“未得到报道”，是科学家的“盲点”。 
进行偶然性工程改造的风险不仅限于animals动物。用于治疗稀有疾病的基因组编辑疗法正在人们身上进行测试，患者最终可能会出现计划外的基因突变。去年在中国首次发生的与基因编辑有关的改变出生前人类儿童的尝试特别令人担忧。
 独立科学家仍然没有机会确认中国儿童（双胞胎女孩）的DNA中是否也有意想不到的错误。世界卫生组织今年表示，任何进一步使基因编辑人员的尝试都是“不负责任的”，部分原因是技术上的不确定性。
  为了生产无角牛，Recombinetics从荷斯坦奶牛的皮肤细胞开始。那就是细菌基因的来源：为了将DNA指令传达给细胞，该公司在2013年开展的工作中采用了质粒-细菌中存在的一种环状微型染色体。
 质粒（仅能暂时保留）带有DNA指令，可用于遗传编辑工具（称为TALENs）和其他品种中发现的“无角”遗传变化，涉及交换约200个遗传字母。后来，经过编辑的皮肤细胞被用于克隆程序中，以产生两只新动物，名为Buri和Spotigy。这些是原始公牛的副本，但没有张扬。
 2015年出生的无角牛很快就成为了基因编辑名人。当Spotigy被杀死以分析其组织时，Buri的寿命足以养育大约17个后代，这两个后代由加利福尼亚大学戴维斯分校的设施和澳大利亚的一个农场分配。四月份，一头无角的母牛为《连线》杂志的封面摆姿势。
 Recombinetics的高管在2017年告诉彭博社：“我们确切地知道该基因应该去哪里，并将其放置在确切的位置。我们拥有的所有科学数据都证明没有脱靶效应。”
  在某种程度上由于不遵守法规，该公司从未寻求过美国无角牛的正式批准。但是，她说，它的加州大学戴维斯分校的兽医科学家艾莉森·范·埃纳纳纳姆（Alison Van Eenennaam）的合作者去年向FDA公开了这些动物的档案，以便与该机构进行交易。 
桑特斯加德（Sontesgard）表示，范·埃纳纳纳姆（Van Eenennaam）随后选择询问戴维斯工厂占位的多余基因编辑动物是否可以进入屠宰场，然后将它们制成牛排和汉堡，从而检验FDA的想法。
 桑特斯加德（Sontesgard）称要赢得“食品豁免权”是为了评估FDA的观点。 “艾莉森很好奇，这些动物是否会被批准作为食物。我说‘好吧，你可以开枪。’”桑特斯加德说。 “如果他们说好的，那将是一个不错的结果，如果他们说不，那么他们将不得不提出合理的推理。我只是不想让他们浪费，因为他们真的没有错。”
 桑特斯加德（Sontesgard）认为，无论有无质粒，这些动物都可以安全食用。
 范·埃纳纳纳姆（Van Eenennaam）考虑了财务因素。焚化重约一吨的实验动物，每磅成本为60美分，她认为这对于她的大学课程来说是一笔不小的开支。最好将它们作为汉堡包出售。
 不过，到了3月，FDA生物信息学家对公牛的基因组进行了测试，结果偶然发现了该质粒的存在-它已以某种方式整合到了布里的基因组中。范·埃纳纳纳姆（Van Eenennaam）说，当月的机构官员安排了一次电话会议，打破了坏消息。无论如何，这些动物都含有细菌基因，并且都是转基因生物。
 Van Eenennaam说：“我们很惊讶，但是当您获得新信息时，您会继续前进。” “科学就是这样做的。”她说，预计布里的一半后代也会遗传细菌基因。
 似乎很难解释为什么该公司和加州大学戴维斯分校（UC Davis）无法看到细菌基因，该细菌基因紧接在动物基因组中的“无角”编辑之后。但是，要研究DNA，研究人员首先必须确定要分离或检查的位，实际上，他们经常在灯光下寻找，这一过程会引入假设和偏见。 
尚不清楚细菌DNA是否构成更大的风险。它不太可能影响母牛或吃牛的人；相反，人们担心的是，抗生素的抗性基因可能会被牛肠或人体中存在的数十亿细菌中的任何一种所吸收。利兹大学（Leeds University）退休的微生物学家约翰·遗产（John Heritage）说，他认为该基因跳跃的可能性不大，但他在牛中的存在可能为它的传播创造了不可预测的机会。
 一些无角动物身上有来自另一种物种的有害DNA的发现，已经封杀了他们的命运。现在没有人会赢得他们的监管批准。 Sontesgard证实，巴西的监管机构已经拒绝了与动物有关的Recombinetics修改后的请愿书。
 范·埃纳纳纳姆（Van Eenennaam）说：“它是新颖的DNA，因此就在整个GMO辩论中投入了它。” “从食品安全的角度来看，这并不令我担心，但从法规角度来说，您甚至都不会尝试。它不是可以向前发展的产品。”
 范·埃纳纳纳姆（Van Eenennaam）说，自从FDA发现以来，加州大学戴维斯分校（UC Davis）焚烧了其农场中五名男性中的三名。摆好《连线》封面的动物已怀孕，暂时将不会被幸免。
note_en:
They were the poster animals for the gene-editing revolution, appearing in  story after  story. By adding just a few letters of DNA to the genomes of dairy cattle, a US startup company had devised a way to make sure the animals never grew troublesome horns.
 To Recombinetics—the St. Paul, Minnesota gene-editing company that made the hornless cattle—the animals were messengers of a new era of better, faster, molecular farming.  “This same outcome could be achieved by breeding in the farmyard,” declared the company's then-CEO Tammy Lee Stanoch in 2017. “This is precision breeding.”
  Food and Drug Administration scientists who had a closer look at the genome sequence of one of the edited animals, a bull named Buri,  have discovered its genome contains a stretch of bacterial DNA including a gene conferring antibiotic resistance.
 The “unintended” addition of DNA from a different species occurred during the gene-editing process itself, the government says. It went undetected by the company even as it touted the animals as 100% bovine and assailed the FDA for saying the animals needed to be regulated at all.
 “It was not something expected, and we didn’t look for it” says Tad Sontesgard, CEO of Acceligen, a subsidiary of Recombinetics that owns the animals. He says a more complete check “should have been done.”
   The blunder is a setback for Recombinetics, whose pioneering prototypes of gene-edited animals include heat-resistant cattle as well as pigs that never hit puberty. It’s also a strike against efforts to make such gene editing a routine practice in animal reproduction. All along, Recombinetics had noisily objected to oversight by the FDA, which categorizes gene-edited animals as new drugs that need extensive testing and approval. It even  lobbied the Trump Administration to wrest oversight away from the health agency, saying it was holding back a barnyard revolution.
 But gene editing isn’t yet as predictable or reliable as promoters say. Instead, the procedure, meant to make pinpoint changes to DNA, can introduce significant unexpected changes without anyone noticing. “As genome-editing technology evolves, so does our understanding of the unintended alterations it produces,” wrote the FDA scientists, led by Alexis Norris and Heather Lombardi, in  a paper they released in July. They think gene-editing errors “are under reported” and a “blind spot” for scientists.
 The risk of haphazard engineering isn’t just to barnyard animals. Genome-editing treatments to cure rare diseases are being tested on people and it is possible that patients will end up with unplanned genetic mutations. Unintended consequences are a particular concern in connection with attempts to modify human children before birth with gene editing—as occurred  for the first time in China last year.
 Independent scientists still haven’t had a chance to confirm whether the Chinese children—twin girls—also have unintended mistakes in their DNA. This year the World Health Organization  said any further attempts to make gene-edited people would be “irresponsible” in part because of technical uncertainty.
  To make its hornless cows, Recombinetics started with skin cells from Holstein dairy bulls. That’s where the bacterial genes came in: to convey DNA instructions to the cell, the company, in work carried out in 2013, employed plasmids—a type of circular mini-chromosome present in bacteria.
 The plasmids (meant to persist only temporarily) carried DNA instructions both for the genetic editing tool (known as TALENs) and the “no horns” genetic change, found in other breeds, and which involves swapping out about 200 genetic letters. Later, edited skin cells were used in a cloning procedure to generate two new animals, named Buri and Spotigy. These were copies of the original bulls, but they didn’t grow horns.
 The hornless cattle, born in 2015, soon became gene-editing celebrities. While Spotigy was killed to analyze his tissues, Buri lived long enough to sire about 17 offspring, which are split between facilities at the University of California, Davis, and a farm in Australia. One hornless daughter cow posed for the cover of  Wired in April.
 “We know exactly where the gene should go, and we put it in its exact location,” Recombinetics executives  told Bloomberg in 2017. “We have all the scientific data that proves that there are no off-target effects.”
  In part because it disagreed with regulations, the company never pursued formal approval for the hornless cattle in the US. However, its collaborator at the University of California, Davis, veterinary scientist Alison Van Eenennaam, opened a file on the animals with the FDA last year, she says, to trade information with the agency.
 According to Sontesgard, Van Eenennaam then chose to test the FDA’s thinking by asking whether some of the surplus gene-edited animals taking up space at the Davis facility could go to a slaughterhouse, where they’d be made into steaks and hamburger.
 Sontesgard calls the bid to win a “food exemption” an attempt to assess the FDA’s views. “Alison was curious whether the animals would be approved as food. I said ‘Okay, you can take a shot,’” says Sontesgard. “If they’d said okay, that would have been a nice outcome, and if they said no, they would have to come up with a logical reasoning. I just didn’t want them to go to waste because there’s really nothing wrong with them.”
 Sontesgard thinks the animals are “safe to eat with or without the plasmid.”
 Van Eenennaam had financial factors in mind. It costs 60 cents a pound to incinerate experimental animals, which weigh about a ton, which she considers a large expense for her university program. Better to sell them as hamburger.
 By March, though, an FDA bioinformatician running tests on the bulls’ genomes had stumbled on the presence of the plasmid—it had somehow integrated into Buri’s genome. Agency officials that month arranged a conference call break the bad news, Van Eenennaam says. The animals contained bacterial genes and were GMOs, by any definition.
 “We were surprised, but when you get new information, you proceed ahead,” says Van Eenennaam. “That is what science does.” She says that half Buri's offspring would be expected to have also inherited the bacteria genes.
 It could seem hard to explain why the company and UC Davis failed to see the bacterial genes, which ended up right next to their ‘no-horns’ edit in the animals’ genomes. However, to study DNA, researchers first have to decide which bits to isolate or check for—in effect, they’re often looking in the lamplight, a process that introduces assumptions and biases, according to the FDA scientists.
 It’s not clear if the bacterial DNA poses a larger risk. It’s unlikely to affect the cow or a person who eats it; instead, the concern is that the antibiotic resistance gene could be taken up by any of the billions of bacteria present in a cow’s gut or body. John Heritage, a retired microbiologist from Leeds University, says he doesn’t see a large chance of the gene jumping further but says its presence in a cow could create unpredictable opportunities for it to spread.
 The discovery that some of the hornless animals have unwanted DNA from another species has sealed their fate. No one is going to win regulatory approval for them now. Already, regulators in Brazil  rejected a revised petition by Recombinetics connected to the animals, Sontesgard confirmed.
 “It’s novel DNA, so right there that throws it into the whole GMO debate,” says Van Eenennaam. “It doesn’t worry me from a food safety perspective, but regulatory—well, you wouldn’t even try. It’s not a product that would move forward.”
 Van Eenennaam says since the FDA discovery, UC Davis has incinerated three of the five males who were living at its farms. The animal who posed for the cover of Wired is pregnant and will be spared for the time being.

title_en: Gene-edited cattle have a major screwup in their DNA

    Google云端服务遇到问题，我们在太平洋标准时间5:30进行其他更新
    Google Cloud Platform和Google Workspace发生了全球性中断，影响了所有需要Google帐户身份验证的服务，持续时间为50分钟。根本原因是我们的自动配额管理系统中的一个问题，该问题减少了Google中央身份管理系统的容量，导致其在全球范围内返回错误。结果，我们无法验证用户请求是否已通过身份验证以及是否向用户提供了错误。
  需要身份验证的GCP服务（包括Cloud Console，Cloud Storage，BigQuery，Google Kubernetes Engine）将为所有用户返回错误。
 需要身份验证的Google Workspace服务（包括Gmail，日历，Meet，文档和云端硬盘）将为所有用户返回错误。
  我们的许多内部用户和工具都遇到过类似的错误，这增加了中断外部通信的延迟。
 完成内部调查后，我们将发布此事件的分析。

    Google Cloud services are experiencing issues and we have an other update at 5:30 PDT
    Google Cloud Platform and Google Workspace experienced a global outage affecting all services which require Google account authentication for a duration of 50 minutes. The root cause was an issue in our automated quota management system which reduced capacity for Google's central identity management system, causing it to return errors globally. As a result, we couldn’t verify that user requests were authenticated and served errors to our users.
  GCP services (including Cloud Console, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Google Kubernetes Engine) requiring authentication would have returned an error for all users.
 Google Workspace services (including Gmail, Calendar, Meet, Docs and Drive) requiring authentication would have returned an error for all users.
  Many of our internal users and tools experienced similar errors, which added delays to our outage external communication.
 We will publish an analysis of this incident once we have completed our internal investigation.
